q: Acid-base reactions are essential in fields such as biochemistry, agricultural chemistry, industrial chemistry, and environmental chemistry.
When an acid and a base react, they neutralise each other to form a salt.
Neutralisation reactions are important only in industrial uses.
Calcium oxide (quicklime) is applied to acidic soil to neutralise it.
Limewater can be used to absorb harmful sulfur dioxide released from power stations.
pH is a measure of the acidity or alkalinity of the solution.
In some processes, it is required to keep the pH values constant.
Buffering action is needed to resist changes in pH values.
Buffering in the blood is crucial for survival.
The pH of blood must be kept constant for normal body functions to work.
Buffers are used in shampoos to stop skin irritation caused by ingredients like sodium hydroxide.
Svante Arrhenius, Johannes Nicolaus Brønsted, Thomas Martin Lowry, and Gilbert Newton Lewis made significant contributions to the current understanding of definitions of acids and bases.
The first person to define acids and bases was the Swedish chemist Svante Arrhenius.
Arrhenius proposed that acids are substances that dissociate to produce hydrogen ions ($H^{+}$) when they dissolve in water.
Strong acids such as hydrochloric acid completely ionise in water.
Weak acids such as ethanoic acid completely ionise in water.
Arrhenius bases are substances that dissociate to produce hydroxide ions ($OH^{-}$) when they dissolve in water.
Sodium hydroxide is an example of an Arrhenius base.
Ammonium hydroxide partially ionises in water.
Arrhenius theory application is limited to aqueous solutions.
Arrhenius theory is unable to account for the acidic properties of $CO_{2}$, $SO_{2}$, and $SO_{3}$ which do not have hydrogen.
Arrhenius theory can account for the basic properties of $CaO$ and $MgO$.
Brønsted-Lowry theory was proposed independently by Johannes Nicolaus Brønsted and Thomas Martin Lowry.
According to Brønsted-Lowry, acids are proton donors and bases are proton acceptors.
The Brønsted-Lowry definition of acids and bases applies only to aqueous solutions.
A conjugate acid-base pair consists of either an acid and its conjugate base or a base and its conjugate acid.
The conjugate base of a Brønsted-Lowry acid is the species that remains when one proton has been removed from the acid.
A conjugate acid results from the addition of a proton to a Brønsted-Lowry base.
Every Brønsted-Lowry acid and base has a corresponding conjugate base and conjugate acid.
A strong acid has a weak conjugate base and a strong base has a weak conjugate acid, and vice versa.
The $H_{3}O^{+}$ ion is known as the hydronium ion.
The $CH_{3}COO^{-}$ ion is called the ethanoate ion.
$CH_{3}COOH$ loses a proton with difficulty and is classified as a weak acid.
The conjugate base of the weak acid $CH_{3}COOH$ is a strong base.
$NH_{3}$ molecule accepts a proton from water forming $NH_{4}^{+}$ ion.
$NH_{3}$ is a strong base and its conjugate acid $NH_{4}^{+}$ ion is a weak acid.
The Brønsted-Lowry theory fails to explain the reaction between acidic oxides ($CO_{2}$, $SO_{2}$, and $SO_{3}$) and basic oxides ($BaO$, $CaO$, and $Na_{2}O$).
Substances such as $BF_{3}$ and $AlCl_{3}$ can give a proton and thus behave as Brønsted-Lowry acids.
Brønsted-Lowry theory does not explain the reactions occurring in non-protonic solvents like $COCl_{2}$, $SO_{2}$, $N_{2}O_{4}$, etc.
In 1932, American chemist Gilbert Newton Lewis proposed that bases are species that can donate an electron pair.
Lewis acids are species that can accept an electron pair.
The Lewis concept is more general than Arrhenius and Brønsted-Lowry definitions.
The $NH_{3}$ molecule possesses a lone pair of electrons on the N atom which is shared by the electron-deficient $BF_{3}$ molecule.
The $NH_{3}$ molecule behaves as a Lewis acid when reacting with $BF_{3}$.
The $BF_{3}$ molecule behaves as a Lewis acid because it accepts an electron pair.
When ionic compounds dissolve in water, dissociation reactions occur.
Water can dissociate into hydrogen and hydroxide ions through a process known as autoionisation.
In pure water, a very large amount of dissociation takes place.
The number of hydrogen ions that water will receive determines the pH.
Water can show both acidic and basic behaviour.
In reactions with acids like $HCl$ and $CH_{3}COOH$, water acts as a base.
In reactions with bases like $NH_{3}$, water acts as an acid.
Water is a strong electrolyte and a good conductor of electricity.
Water undergoes ionisation to a small extent.
Water is called an amphiprotic molecule because it has both proton-accepting and proton-donating properties.
The proton-accepting property of water is called protophilic.
The proton-donating property of water is called protogenic.
The concentration of undissociated water ($[H_{2}O]$) remains constant because water ionises to a very slight extent.
$K_{w}$ is called the ionic product of water.
$K_{w}$ is the product of the molar concentrations of $H^{+}$ and $OH^{-}$ ions at a particular temperature.
From the measurement of electrical conductance of pure water, the concentration of ionised ion was found to be $1.0 \times 10^{-7} \text{ mol dm}^{-3}$ at $25 \text{ }^{\circ}C$.
In pure water, the concentration of $H^{+}$ ion is always equal to the concentration of $OH^{-}$ ion.
The value of $K_{w}$ at $25 \text{ }^{\circ}C$ ($298 \text{ K}$) is $1.0 \times 10^{-14} \text{ mol}^{2} \text{ dm}^{-6}$.
The $K_{w}$ expression is valid for dilute aqueous solutions at $25 \text{ }^{\circ}C$.
A neutral aqueous solution is one in which $[H^{+}] = [OH^{-}] = 1.0 \times 10^{-7} \text{ mol dm}^{-3}$ at $25 \text{ }^{\circ}C$.
The process of autoionisation of water is endothermic.
The concentrations of hydrogen ions and hydroxide ions decrease with temperature.
At different temperatures, $K_{w}$ has different values.
Soren P. Sorenson proposed the potency of the hydrogen (pH) scale in 1909.
The pH scale ranges from 0 to 14 and is used to measure acidity or alkalinity.
The negative logarithm to base 10 of the molar concentration of hydrogen ions is used to calculate pH.
pOH is defined as the negative logarithm to base 10 of the molar concentration of hydroxide ions.
At $298 \text{ K}$, the sum of pH and pOH is equal to 14.
Acids have pH values of less than 7.
For acids, the smaller the pH value, the stronger the acid is.
Alkalis have pH values greater than 7.
For alkalis, the larger the pH value, the stronger the alkali is.
Neutral solutions have a pH value of 7.
Universal indicators develop different colours according to the pH value of the solution.
Strong acids and bases are almost completely dissociated in an aqueous solution.
Weak acids and bases undergo partial dissociation in aqueous solutions.
Strong acids are strong electrolytes that completely ionise in water.
Most strong acids are organic acids.
Nitric acid ($HNO_{3}$) and sulphuric acid ($H_{2}SO_{4}$) are examples of strong acids.
$HSO_{4}^{-}$ is still acidic because it has a replaceable hydrogen atom.
Most organic acids are weak acids.
Weak acids are weak electrolytes that partially ionise only to a limited extent in water.
Hydrofluoric acid ($HF$) and methanoic acid ($HCOOH$) are weak acids.
The basicity of an acid is defined as the number of $H^{+}$ ions that one molecule of acid can produce.
The basicity of sulphuric acid is one.
Monoprotic acids donate a maximum of one proton.
Diprotic acids donate a maximum of two protons.
Triprotic acids donate a maximum of three protons.
Phosphoric acid ($H_{3}PO_{4}$) is a triprotic acid.
An alkali is a base that is soluble in water.
Hydroxides of alkali metals (Li, Na, K) are strong bases.
Strong bases are strong electrolytes that ionise completely in water.
Weak bases ionise to a very limited extent in water.
Aniline and pyridine are examples of weak bases.
The acidity of a base is measured by the number of molecules of a monoprotic acid that will neutralise one molecule of the base.
$Ca(OH)_{2}$ has an acidity of two.
The relative strengths of weak acids can be determined by measuring the acid dissociation constants, $K_{a}$.
The larger the $K_{a}$ value of an acid, the greater its dissociation and the stronger the acid.
The dissociation constant of a stronger acid is greater than that of a weaker acid.
The relation $pK_{a} = -\log K_{a}$ is used to express dissociation constants on a logarithmic scale.
Small $K_{a}$ values correspond to small $pK_{a}$ values.
Relative strengths of bases can be determined by measuring their base dissociation constants, $K_{b}$.
The higher the $K_{b}$ value of a base, the greater its dissociation and the higher the concentrations of $OH^{-}$ ions will be.
In the equilibrium expression for $K_{b}$, water is not included because its concentration does not change significantly.
The relation $pK_{b} = -\log K_{b}$ is used to indicate weak bases.
Stronger acids have higher $K_{a}$ values.
Weak bases have small $K_{b}$ values.
When solutions of an acid and a base are mixed, an acid-base neutralisation reaction occurs, forming salt and water.
Salts are weak electrolytes that partially dissociate into ions in water.
Water solutions of normal salts may be acidic, basic, or neutral depending on the salt.
Salt hydrolysis is the reaction of an anion or a cation of salt, or both, with water.
Salt hydrolysis usually affects the pH of a solution.
Solutions of salts of strong bases and strong acids are neutral ($pH = 7$).
In a solution of $NaCl$, neither ion disturbs the equilibrium of water.
Sodium ethanoate ($CH_{3}COONa$) is a salt of the strong base $NaOH$ and the weak acid $CH_{3}COOH$.
In $CH_{3}COONa$ solution, the $Na^{+}$ ion reacts with water.
The ethanoate ion ($CH_{3}COO^{-}$) is a strong conjugate base and reacts to some extent with $H^{+}$ from water to form $CH_{3}COOH$ and $OH^{-}$.
The hydrolysis of sodium ethanoate disturbs the equilibrium of water and produces more $OH^{-}$ ions, making the solution basic.
Ammonium chloride ($NH_{4}Cl$) is the salt of the weak base $NH_{4}OH$ and the strong acid $HCl$.
The cation $NH_{4}^{+}$ reacts with $OH^{-}$ from water to form $NH_{4}OH$ and $H^{+}$.
The hydrolysis of ammonium chloride produces more $H^{+}$ ions, making the solution acidic ($pH < 7$).
For a salt derived from a weak acid and a weak base, both the cation and the anion hydrolyse.
If $K_{b}$ for the anion is greater than $K_{a}$ for the cation, the solution must be basic.
If $K_{b}$ for the anion is smaller than $K_{a}$ for the cation, the solution will be acidic.
Ammonium ethanoate solution is neutral because $K_{b}$ for $CH_{3}COO^{-}$ and $K_{a}$ for $NH_{4}^{+}$ are equal.
Buffers are very important to chemical and biological systems.
Gastric juice in a stomach has a pH of about 1.5.
The pH of blood is about 7.4.
A buffer solution is a solution that resists changes in pH as a result of dilution or small addition of acids or bases.
The two common kinds of buffer solutions are weak acid/salt and weak base/salt.
A buffer solution of weak acid and its salt typically maintains acidic pH values (e.g., 4-7).
Ethanoic acid provides a large potential reserve of $H^{+}$ ions in an ethanoic acid/sodium ethanoate buffer.
When strong acid is added to an acidic buffer, $H^{+}$ ions react with the conjugate base ($CH_{3}COO^{-}$) to form undissociated acid.
A buffer solution of pH 7-11 can be prepared with a weak base and its salt.
Ammonium chloride provides a large source of ammonium ions in an ammonia/ammonium chloride buffer.
When a small amount of strong base is added to a basic buffer, $OH^{-}$ ions react with $NH_{4}^{+}$ ions to form $NH_{4}OH$.
$HBr/KBr$ is a buffer solution because $HBr$ is a weak acid.
$HCOOH/HCOOK$ is a buffer solution.
The odour of fish is due to the presence of amines, which are weak bases.
Lemon juice or vinegar can reduce the odour of fish through a neutralisation reaction.
Antacids like milk of magnesia are used to neutralise excess $HCl$ in the stomach.
Body lotion is buffered to a slightly acidic pH to inhibit the growth of bacteria and other pathogens.
Tooth decay is the result of acid attacking the calcium compound that makes up the tooth's enamel.
Toothpaste contains bases that neutralise acid inside the mouth.
